data.title: My Dividend Stock Portfolio: New January Dividend Record - 100 Holdings With 14 Buys
data.description:
Portfolio Changes in January

Investment activity in January remained consistent with previous months as I added around $1,500 in net capital with almost all of that allocated to Business Development Companies.

My main picks were the Owl Rock Capital (OBDC), Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP) and W.P. Carey (WPC).

In January, all my moves added $126 to my yearly dividend income to start off the new year with a solid level. My aim is to lift my dividend income by about $100 each month, mostly doable thanks to the high yields from BDCs, and I am currently far ahead of that goal.

Overall, the average yield on cost of my new investments in January averaged around 8.5%, reinforcing my belief in BDCs as a core part of my portfolio. I'm banking on their dividends staying steady - that's what I'm all about when it comes to generating income. Time will tell if I'm on the money or just overly optimistic.

All these purchases break down as follows:

All net purchases in January can be found below:

Dividend Income in January 2025

Dividend income hit a new blistering January record of $814. This represents growth of 21% Y/Y and 8% sequentially. These figures are more than impressive to me and mostly boosted by the relatively recent aggressive additions of Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL). My intention is to continue to build these positions into the $5,000 to $10,000 range and ideally reach a level of around $1,000 in dividends per position per year as a mid-to-long-term goal.

In alignment with my investment strategy, I've incorporated a BDC/Non-BDC breakdown into my dividend income charts. This addition allows for more precise monitoring of this critical development.

Additionally, I have created a new visualization that dissects dividend income into two distinct categories: BDCs and Non-BDCs. These categories in turn are separated by dividend payment frequency into Monthly (blue) and Quarterly (orange) in absolute and relative terms.

For Q1 2024, BDCs accounted for only 22% of my dividend income making it a quarter that is not focused on BDCs. That is expected to drop further in February and then likely double in March where most of the BDCs are paying including any special dividends and Hercules Capital which follows an irregular dividend schedule.

This metric provides valuable insights and will help guide my investment decisions as it evolves over time.

In January, dividend income from my top three regular payers reached $260, a big jump from last year's average of $233 and getting closer to the $300 level. This is mainly driven by ongoing purchases of W. P. Carey (WPC), Rithm Capital (RITM), Goldman Sachs BDC, Altria (MO) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund.

All this is depicted below:

Here is a chart that shows the development of my net dividend income by month over time between 2015 and 2025. You can see the growth of my dividend income and the average annual dividend for each year:

This chart is my favorite because it illustrates the progression of my dividend income over time and allows me to easily see the average annual dividend for each year.

Next, I have plotted all the individual dividend payments I have received, coloring them by year and arranging the years side by side instead of horizontally as in previous updates. This visualization allows for a more comprehensive analysis of the distribution of my dividend payments over time.

By visualizing the data in this manner, we get a clear picture of how dividend payments are spread out over different years, offering valuable insights into the overall growth of my dividend income over the years.

This visualization is initially quite cluttered, but it contains a wealth of information. It shows every individual dividend payment I have received since I began my investment journey in 2015, represented as a circle that is colored and sized according to its contribution. The view is organized by month and year, allowing for a more comprehensive analysis of the development of my dividend income over time. For each year and month, a white rectangle indicates the average monthly dividend. The area where dividends fall below the average is filled in dark red, while the area above the average is colored dark green.

This type of data visualization allows for a detailed examination of the distribution of my dividend payments over time and enables me to easily identify trends and patterns in my income growth.

Now, zooming in on January, we can immediately see a number of big dark blue circles in a sea of black. The bigger the circle and the bigger the distance to the previous circle for the same stock, the bigger is the change in dividend income compared to the prior years.

The importance of W.P. Carey, Goldman Sachs BDC, Rithm Capital and Altria is very clear, with a reddish circle indicating the massive jump in dividend income over the last 12 months due to heavy ongoing monthly investments, and I have no plans to stop them either via monthly savings plans or on an ad-hoc basis up to several times a week.

Apart from that, when it comes to the dividends for 2025, I notice mostly blue circles at the top of the scale, which is exactly what I want to see. These big blue circles represent a rising dividend income, and my goal is to have as many of them as possible at the top of the chart.

Overall, my aim is to observe numerous large orange circles positioned at the highest point on the scale. This signifies a positive trend of increasing dividend income, which is precisely what I'm striving for.

Another way to express the monthly dividend income is in terms of Gifted Working Time (GWT). Assuming an average hourly rate of $37.34 for 2025, my GWT and annual net dividends have been as follows:

  • 2018: 121 hours GWT, $3,000 in annual net dividends
  • 2019: 142 hours GWT, $3,600 in annual net dividends
  • 2020: 152 hours GWT, $3,800 in annual net dividends
  • 2021: 180 hours GWT, $5,050 in annual net dividends
  • 2022: 229 hours GWT, $6,400 in annual net dividends
  • 2023: 272 hours GWT, $8,800 in annual net dividends
  • 2024: 284 hours GWT, $10,200 in annual net dividends
  • 2025: Targeting at least 295 hours GWT, $11,000 in annual net dividends

The overall target for the year with $11,000 in annual net dividends is very conservative, but I expect that I will have to take out some risk from my portfolio as I am eyeing to raise capital for real estate investments if I encounter the right deal and definitely for a lot of traveling. This would represent a growth rate of around 8% which admittedly is a low target but definitely something I am willing to update as the year unfolds. If things go differently the dream target would be to pass the next milestone of $12,000 which would require around 18% dividend growth.

That would be a great achievement but nothing I am banking on to reach this year - it would be the icing on the cake.

Across the years, my YTD Dividend Race has been as follows:

  • 2018: Disappointing
  • 2019: Phenomenal, benefiting from a low baseline in the prior year
  • 2020: Fairly disappointing
  • 2021: Phenomenal
  • 2022: Much better than expected, fueled by the very weak euro
  • 2023: Breathtaking
  • 2024: Simply great

I have updated the YTD Dividend Race chart to show the development of YTD dividend income on a daily basis, allowing us to see that most of my dividend income is generated mid-month and towards the end of the month. The chart also breaks down the development by month for the current year, making it easy to identify significant jumps in income.

Expressed in GWT, it presents itself as follows:

What this shows is as follows:

  1. All time (blue area) - Around 1,514 hours, or 189 days, of active work have been replaced with passive income since the start of my dividend journey. Assuming a five-day workweek, this translates into more than half a year in total.
  2. Full-year 2025 (green bars) - Around 22 hours, or 2.7 days, of active work have been replaced with passive income in 2025, which is basically equivalent to half a working week funded with dividends. That is great progress, and I can't wait to add another month to that statistic.
  3. Highlighted in pink is the accumulated total at the end of the current reporting month (January).

This visualization allows us to see the overall impact of my dividend income on the amount of active work I am able to replace with passive income. It also enables us to see the progress made year-to-date, as well as the accumulated total at the end of the current reporting month.

Upcoming February Dividends

Contrary to January, February features significantly fewer dividend payers but among them are the big dividends from AT&T (T) and AbbVie (ABBV). Beyond that dividend payments are overall concentrated in the first half of the month with only one meaningful dividend payment from the Royal Bank of Canada (RY) expected in the second half of February.

The snapshot below is taken from my free-for-all released Dividend Calendar (make sure to follow instructions in the video) and shows my expected dividend payments in February.

At the end of January, my dividend portfolio is composed as follows:

data.title: Kellanova: Nowhere To Go, No Value To Grow, Hold
data.description:
You are all probably familiar with the news that Mars Incorporated (a non-publicly traded company) is to acquire Kellanova (NYSE:K) for $83.50 per share. But what if the acquisition doesn't go through... Well, that is what I want to focus on in this article. I will analyze the company and explain the reasons to why I believe that if the acquisition doesn't go through, they are most likely to never in the future reach values of above $80 a share, and on the contrary be a pit hole for investors.

The first thing you have to understand is that as Mars is not publicly traded, if this acquisition does go through, every investor's position will be liquidated, and they will receive a payment of $83.50 for every share they own. This means that until the acquisition goes through fully, the company will never reach values of more than the said $83.50, as that would leave investors with a certain loss.

This acquisition was announced on the 14th of August 2024 and is the sole reason why the shares of the company soared from about $57 a share to $80 a share, south of a 50% increase in a matter of two weeks. So currently, trading at around $82 a share, they show little to no movement as investors are mostly betting on that the acquisition will go through. But again, what if it doesn't...

So jumping right in, as always to analyze the company, we will go through the 9 Pillar analysis and by the end of the article, calculate the company's intrinsic value using our 4 valuation models.

9 Pillar Analysis

First as always, if you are not familiar with the 9 Pillars, let me explain what they are for. Pillar 1 and 2 show a company's PE and P/FCF movement over the past 5 years, with pillars 3,4 and 5 showing the 5-year revenue, net income and FCF growth. Pillars one and two suggest the value of the company, while 3,4 and 5 depict its growth.

Regarding pillar 6, that focuses on the trailing 5-year profitability, with pillar 7 focusing on the 5-year shares outstanding change. Pillar 8 focuses on the dividends, and pillar 9 helps me understand how indebted a company is. That is essentially it, a very simple but effective tool to value stocks.

Analysis

Nowhere to Go, No Value to Grow

Now... Let's first take a look at the financial growth (Pillars 1,2,3). Over the past 5 years, the revenue has on average been decreasing at a rate of 1.22% yearly. The net income has actually increased from FY20 where it sat at $1.25B, to $1.34B in FY24, and the FCF, like the revenue has on average been decreasing at a rate of 5.25% YOY.

Last Quarter's Earnings

This doesn't broadcast anything positive. Furthermore, In the latest FY24'Q4 Earnings call which happened on the 6th of February, the company's North American sector which accounts for about 50% of all sales reported a decrease in net sales by 2% YOY.

On the other hand, the operating profit increased by 28%, with the adjusted operating profit increasing by 8%, and by 9% excluding currency translation.

Regarding the international sector of the company, which accounts for the other 50% of their sales, Europe FY24'Q4 net sales decreased by 2%, with the operating profit increasing by 24%, Asia Pacific, Middle East and Africa's (AMEA) net sales increased by 2% and operating profit increased by 47%.

So overall the company is increasing its profitability, but despite that, their financials are still mostly falling.

Long-Term Return

ROIC

Revenue

Net Income

With that said, Consumer Staples (Kellanova's sector) isn't at all a fast grower. In fact, over the past decade, Consumer Staples have heavily underperformed the SP500, with it being up by 189.79% compared to the mentioned sector being up on average by 28.64%.

That in mind, with their falling financial, in my eyes the risk heavily outweighs the reward.

Dividend Growth

But let's continue and take a look at the dividend growth (pillar 8).

And... This isn't looking too great either. From 2020 the dividend per share has decreased from $2.28 to $2.26 in 2024, which accounts for an average YOY decrease of 0.20% through the past 5 years.

This is actually something I expected as with their decreasing FCF we can't really expect an increase in the dividends at a significant amount, if at all.

Decreasing Value

Something I also do not like is the fact that over the past 5 years, Kellanova hasn't been buying back any shares whatsoever, which is something I wouldn't expect from a well established company such as themselves. From 2020 the shares outstanding have on yearly bases been increasing at a rate of 0.06% and are currently sitting at 346M, about a million higher than in 2020.

This is of course a bummer, as by not decreasing their shares outstanding the company doesn't allow their EPS and FCF/S to increase, and therefore the company's value growth is slowed down. In fact, with Kellanova's decreasing FCF, it is actually decreasing.

For those of you wondering why I inspect the FCF/S change instead of the EPS, it is because FCF is essentially used for everything; Share buybacks, dividend increases, paying down debt etc... while the net income is essentially just a measuring metric.

Below, you can see the fast decreasing FCF/S over the past years, which quite clearly depicts the value decrease in the company.

Valuation

Now to get to the actual valuation of the company, and so to pillars 1 and 2, the PE and P/FCF ratios. It is quite clear that with the rapid increase in stock price in the first half of August 2024 for no other reason but the possible acquisition, the two ratios had to increase as well.

The PE increased from an average of 16.24 in 2023 to 21.19 in 2024 while the P/FCF jumped to 29.26 from 19.27 in 2023, about a 30% increase. Both of these ratios are much higher than the sectors average, and so when comparing Kellanova to its peers in the Multiples valuation; The Hershey Company (NYSE:HSY), General Mills (NYSE:GIS) and Kraft Heinz (NASDAY:KHC), I got an intrinsic value of $57.32, which is around 44.80% lower than the current price of $82.20.

So this is another thing that bothers me, as in my eyes it is quite clear that if the acquisition was to not go through, the stock price would fall back to pre-August levels.

This belief of mine is also supported by three other valuations I have carried out; the Graham's Formula, DCF model and the DDM.

For the Graham's formula, I predicted the 5Y growth at 4.23% per annum, in line with their historical net income growth. Based on the historical 5Y EPS, and the current Yield on AAA corp. bonds, after plugging in my projected growth rate into the Graham's formula, I got an intrinsic value of $43.44, 47% lower than the current trading price.

Regarding the DCF model, I predicted the long term FCF growth at 2.7% (quiet bullish for their current negative FCF growth) and plugged in a very low discount rate based on the WACC (weighted average cost of capital) of the company at 6.13%, essentially my desired YOY return. This gave me an intrinsic value of $73.67, lower then the current price even with the the high FCF growth predicted.

Finally, from the dividend discount model I got an intrinsic value of $46.51 based on a YOY 1.17% predicted dividend growth rate.

After averaging out the 4 valuations, I got an intrinsic value of $55.24 which is 32.80% lower than the current price.

And with that let me present my Bull and Bear cases...

The Future

Bears

If the acquisition doesn't go through, in my eyes it is practically certain that the stock price will fall by close to 30% (based on its overvaluation) if not more, and with the company's 5Y revenue growth sitting at negative 1.22%, and the FCF falling by an average of 5.25% YOY, it would take them decades to reach a value of $82.00, if they would ever manage.

Even with the 5Y net income CAGR sitting at 4.75%, I am bearish on the current valuation based on the trailing EPS of $3.48. Which based on the Graham's formula is quite lower that what would be needed for the company to at their current price trade at a fair valuation.

This brings me onto my next point, Kellanova faces intense competition from private labels, which are often cheaper and well marketed. Expected to grow at a CAGR of 10.8% reaching a value of $2.05 Trillion in 2032, while ultra-processed foods are expected to grow at a CAGR of 9% from 2025 to 2029.

Below, you can see the expected growth of private labels, over the next 8 years.

The company is furthermore faced with a new reality, where the preferences of its customers is turning from ultra processed foods to healthier alternatives.

Furthermore, over the past years after Covid-19 struck and prices climbed higher with inflation at all-time highs, many consumers cut their spendings, focusing on cheaper branded products.

On the graph below, you can see the decrease in the average US household percentage of income spent on food at home

Bulls

Now getting to the bulls... The only bull case I see is if Kellanova does get acquired by Mars Incorporated, and that way long-term investors would get their value back in cash and all would be well.

Conclusion

So at last we have reached the end. With the rising sentiment to live healthier, the rise of branded products and changing consumer habits, I believe that it is only good that Kellanova is getting acquired, as this is the only way that investors could receive a value significantly higher than their initial investment.

As I do believe that the acquisition will go through I give the company a HOLD rating, but if it weren't for a reason I would certainly rate it a SELL, as I do not see it growing in the next decades.

data.title: Vale: How Solid Is The Dividend Thesis
data.description:
For dividend-seekers, arguably, the Brazil-based mining giant Vale (NYSE:VALE) remains a compelling pick.

I have, throughout my last few articles covering Vale, adopted a bullish stance on the company based mainly on its very high returns to its shareholders (through dividends and buybacks) as well as its severe undervaluation considering its fundamentals.

Although Vale is a cyclical company, i.e., highly vulnerable and connected to major fluctuations in iron ore prices—which are mostly impacted by the dynamics of China's economy—there is significant volatility in Vale's performance amid these cycles.

However, in this article, I will provide ten fundamentalist criteria, based on the book The Little Book of Big Dividends by Charles B. Carlson, which support Vale as an attractive and consistent dividend payer, even in the midst of the cyclicality of its business.

I continue to believe that investors who take advantage of iron ore's current downward cycle by buying Vale at current prices, even with a not necessarily compelling outlook for 2025, tend to make a good deal.

Payout

The first is the payout. Based on the premise that dividend seekers should only invest in companies that have a sustainable payout, we can see that Vale is indeed one of them.

Over the last ten years, Vale has, on average, paid out half of its profits in dividends. Although in 2020, the Brazilian iron ore giant paid out more than it profited, this was a point outside the curve.

But the main point here is that Vale has, in fact, been sustainable in its payout, even though it is a cyclical company, which, in my view, qualifies as the first point for being a good income stock.

Interest Coverage

When we look at Vale's interest coverage, i.e., how much interest expense the company has compared to its operating profit, we can see that Vale is also doing more than fine.

This is based on its operating profit (EBIT) of $12.65 billion in the last twelve months and an interest expense of $1.2 billion. In other words, in this case, we have an interest coverage ratio of over 10.5x, which is extremely solid.

As a benchmark, dividend-focused investors should look for companies with a stable and high interest coverage ratio (typically above 3- 4x).

See also how over the last decade, despite the cyclicality, Vale has also achieved very robust interest coverage ratios.

Cash Flow to Net Income

Vale generated $8.85 billion in the last twelve months in net income, $10.40 billion in cash from operations, and slightly less unleveraged free cash flow, standing at $8.12 billion.

In this case, seeking consistency between cash flow and earnings to ensure quality, a ratio greater than 1x is desired.

When we divide Vale's $10.4 billion in cash from operations with its $8.85 billion in net income, we arrive at 1.18x, i.e., Vale has generated 18% more cash flow than its profits, showing that its profits are backed by real cash inflows.

The most important thing is that for a cyclical company like Vale, this is even more reassuring because it means they have enough cash to weather downturns, fund dividends, and manage capital expenses even when net income fluctuates.

Dividend Yield

When it comes to dividend stock investing, yield is considered by many to be the most important factor. The yield is what investors get to pocket relative to how much the stock is currently worth in the market.

Let's look at Vale's dividends (although Seeking Alpha stats show that it doesn't pay dividends). The company did distribute $5.63 billion in dividends over the last twelve months, and with 4.269 billion outstanding shares, we get a dividend per share of $1.32 over the last twelve months. Divided by the share price of $9.76, at last check, this means a dividend yield of a robust 13.5%.

Given that Vale is a Brazilian company, it’s important to keep in mind its exposure to the risks tied to the local economy. I always like to compare this percentage with the country's equity risk premium. In this case, Brazil's equity risk premium currently stands at 7.67%, making Vale's current yield very attractive compared to the intrinsic risks of investing in a Brazilian company.

Looking further ahead, if we consider analysts' projections where they anticipate Vale generating $1.94 EPS in 2025, if we conservatively consider an average payout of 52%, that would mean $1 in dividends per share, which at the current share price implies a yield of 10.44%, still quite attractive and well above the equity risk premium.

I believe, however, that Vale is capable of paying a dividend per share higher than this. The company has consistently repurchased its shares, taking almost 16% of its shares out of circulation in the last five years and, more specifically, $417.2 million over the past twelve months.

Book Value Growth

The fifth indicator is Vale's book value.

Considering that Vale is a cyclical company, in periods of higher iron ore prices, the company has managed to grow its book value more robustly on a quarterly basis. Despite being somewhat stable at around $40 billion since 2020, in the last three years, Vale has managed to grow its book value, even with greater volatility in iron ore prices due to the economic contraction in China.

Long-term Earnings Growth

The next criterion is the evolution of net profit. Over the last decade, we can see that Vale increased its net income substantially from 2020 to 2022.

One may correctly say that since its peak in 2022, Vale's net income has been free-falling. But in my view, this is not how a cyclical company should be analyzed. It may be inaccurate to compare a cycle average like we see today, with iron ore at a current price of $106 (US$/t), with the profit Vale had when the ore was above US$200/t.

Above US$200/t can be considered a very high price, as below US$70/t may be too low. The big question is, is Vale having upward tops and bottoms over time in its profitability?

In Vale's case, by reaching the last bottom in 2024, the trend shows that it is.

Cash Flow Growth, Dividend Growth Rate, and Consistency in Dividend Payments

The last three analysis criteria need a little adaptation for Vale's case.

The model inspired by Charles B. Carlson's Value approach suggests analyzing the last three years of growth in cash flow, dividends, and profit, but when we look at this period, we see that these indicators have fallen.

The problem is that three years ago, in 2021 and 2022, Vale was in a moment of extreme profitability due to the strong rise in iron ore, more specifically driven by both the pandemic and the war between Russia and Ukraine.

Time has passed quickly, and almost three years have passed since then. As a result, when we analyze these three indicators, we see that they are in a downtrend.

But once again, considering that Vale is a cyclical company, looking at a long-cycle period of 10 years, it again shows rising tops and bottoms in dividends, cash flow, and profitability.

Therefore, I consider making an addendum to each of these criteria, not totally exonerating the inconsistency of these indicators of Vale but giving a discount because the original parameters of the analysis are not completely adequate for a cyclical company.

Valuation, The Additional Criterion

Although valuations are not listed in Charles B. Carlson's book to help identify high-quality dividend-paying stocks that offer strong and stable returns, I believe there is little point in buying a good dividend-payer at a price that is disconnected from your reality.

In Vale's case, I still perceive that the current share price is severely undervalued.

Vale trades at a forward P/E ratio of a mere 5x and a forward EV/EBITDA of 3.5x, both well below the industry averages of 15x and 8x, respectively.

Furthermore, when we use the Gordon growth model, which is essentially designed to value the equity in a stable firm paying dividends, Vale also appears to be quite undervalued.

Considering a forward EPS of $1.94 per share, a (conservative) payout of 52%, a cost of equity of 8.78% (based on a 0.92 five-year beta, 7.4% risk-free rate, and 7.67% risk premium), and an expected growth rate of 2%, we arrive at a share price of $15.17 for Vale, i.e., an upside potential of 55% from current prices.

Risks and Final Remarks

Needless to say, iron ore prices can completely change Vale's outlook, and arguably, this is the main reason why I believe the company has been valued so conservatively by the market. However, I believe that a longer-term approach, looking at Vale's performance in different iron ore price scenarios, shows that even so, the Brazilian company managed to generate an improvement in its most crucial metrics to the delight of income-oriented investors.

It is also interesting to note that, according to the company's own projections, if the price of iron ore rises above US$100/t, Vale projects a free cash flow yield in 2026 of 12%, meaning consistent dividends in sight.

In other words, the bottom line is that Vale is a cyclical company, so if the price of ore falls, the dividend will naturally fall, too.

My recommendation is to calibrate exposure to Vale according to volatility tolerance, as it is a more volatile company than an average typical income stock. With that said, I see Vale as a buy-and-hold opportunity at current prices, which, based on its dividend distribution, looks like a great chance to generate alpha over the next few years.

data.title: Rates Spark: Finding Room For Increased Defence Spending
data.description:
By Benjamin Schroeder, Michiel Tukker

Markets focus on joint EU borrowing for defence spending

10Y Bund yields notched higher on Tuesday as bond markets weighed the prospect of rising debt issuance. Although joint European Union borrowing options are being contemplated, the EU Commission has also been mulling activating national escape clauses to its fiscal rules to allow for defence spending beyond the prescribed sovereign borrowing limits. Economy Commissioner Valdis Dombrovskis said a proposal would be presented in the “coming weeks” and indicated that the exceptions could remain in place for "several years".

Taking the 2024 spending levels as a starting point, the countries that have the most catching up to do to even reach NATO’s current 2% target level are the periphery countries Spain, Italy, Portugal, and Belgium. Even in countries that have met the 2% criteria, significantly higher defence spending appears to be in store - on top of any other investment backlogs that have been identified. Looking at Germany’s debt metrics, it is probably one of the few issuers with the fiscal room to stem additional funding needs. For a country like Italy, the EU forecasted last November that the debt-to-GDP ratio could approach 140% in the coming years.

For now, the tightening of eurozone sovereign spreads points to a market that is focusing more on the push towards additional joint borrowing, narrowing 10y Italian spreads over Bunds to their tightest since 2021. The prospect of higher Italian sovereign issuance seems to have taken a back seat amid hopes for the bloc moving ever closer together.

The front end of the EUR curve continues to look more anchored, with markets still priced for the European Central Bank reaching 2% in the deposit facility rates by the end of the year. On Tuesday, the ECB’s Piero Cippolone made the point that the central bank's rate-setting would also have to take into account its shrinking balance sheet amid quantitative tightening - specifically also its impact on long-end yields, which we would interpret as a clear nod to the swap spread developments, e.g., in Bunds. In our money market outlook, we also look at the implications for very front-end spreads.

Today's events and market view

UK CPI came in mixed this morning with headline inflation hotter than anticipated, but core and services in line to slightly cooler. No notable data is expected from the eurozone, and from the US we only have housing starts and building permits. From the FOMC, we do receive the minutes from January’s meeting later in the day.

In terms of supply, from Italy we have a third day of open books for 8Y BTP Valore retail bonds. Monday saw €5.6bn worth of subscriptions and Tuesday €3.6bn. Slovakia has announced a syndication for new 15Y SLOVGBs, for an estimated €2-3bn. Germany has slated a 10Y Bund auction for €4.5bn. From the UK we have a 3Y gilt auction totalling £4.25bn, and lastly from the US a new 20Y Bond for $1.2bn.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Traton: A Truck Titan Ready To Prosper But Stay On The Sidelines (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Who would have thought that a way to beat the market in the last three months would have been investing in an industrial, specifically a truck manufacturer? Well, since late November 2024, shareholders of Traton SE experienced 20+% gains vs. the S&P's meager 1.9%. Traton (OTCPK:TRATY)(OTCPK:TRATF) will release its FY24 Annual Report on March 10, but we already have an important document available (the preliminary release) that helps us assess how the company performed and what this year may look like for truck manufacturers.

Traton Fighting For The Top

First of all, a few words on Traton, since it may be a company not everyone knows about. It was formed in 2019, when Volkswagen spun it off to raise cash for its huge investment needs as it prepared for the EV turnaround. In 2021, Traton completed the acquisition of the American truck manufacturer Navistar, paying $3.7B and delisting it from public trade. The group owns and operates big brands in Europe, such as the Swedish Scania and MAN. In South America, it mainly sells through the brand Volkswagen Truck & Bus.

A few years ago, Traton faced the issue of an unprofitable MAN and below-market margins. At the same time, it held a premium brand such as Scania, whose RoS is constantly in the low teens. Well, Traton's turnaround has been nothing but spectacular, and the company has recently reached double-digit territory, where only the industry leaders (Paccar and Volvo Group) are found. MAN reported a RoS between 5.5% and 7.5%, which represents a noteworthy achievement, considering the starting point. There is still room to grow since Traton has not yet fully focused on developing a service business following the path traced by Paccar and Volvo. Moreover, Traton has positioned itself well in the BEV market, with rising orders in a depressed market.

And yet, enthusiasm should be moderated, since 2024 was a year that posed some challenges to the industry as a whole. Demand for new trucks softened, both because of postponed investments by carriers and because of reduced government funding (the time of stimulus checks eventually came to an end). Hence, Traton warned us that 3Q should be considered a peak margin quarter.

At the end of its 3Q24 call, Traton delivered its final 2024 guidance, saying it was expecting sales to grow between 5% and 10%. The expected RoS was forecasted to be in the range of 9% to 10%. Traton guided for EUR 2.3B in industrial FCF, an EUR 700M reduction YoY. Moreover, Traton informed investors that 2025 will be a year where capital will be allocated to reduce debt. This will impact Traton's dividend because its policy is to pay between 30% and 40% of its net income as a dividend. So, it might be possible that we won't see a dividend increase this year.

Traton's Q4 Earnings Preview

Now, let's look at the preliminary data that Traton released to gauge more or less how FY24 looked like and infer any information regarding FY25.

First of all, unit sales were down only 1% YoY. The poor performance of MAN (-18% YoY) was almost offset by Scania (+6%) and Volkswagen Truck & Bus (+23%). The strong market in South America, therefore, turned out to be an important source of orders and revenues that move according to its cycles rather than being strictly linked to the Western countries. Noteworthy was Navistar, now called International, whose unit sales increased 2% YoY in a declining North American truck market.

If we use the 9M24 data, we see that Scania sold 74k units and made EUR 13.9B in revenues. This translates into an ASP of EUR 188k. MAN sold 69k units and reported revenues of EUR 10.1B, which leads us to an ASP of EUR 146k. International sold 67k trucks and grossed EUR 8.3B for an ASP of EUR 124K. Volkswagen Truck and Bus reported 36K units sold and EUR 2.3B in revenues, an ASP of EUR 65k.

So, if we use the ASPs calculated above, we can make an educated guess about Traton's FY24 revenues.

units sold 9M24 ASP FY24E revenue (in EUR billions)
Scania 102,100 188,000 19.2
MAN 95,700 146,000 14.0
International 90,600 124,000 11.2
VW Truck & Bus 45,800 65,000 3.0

We have a total of EUR 47.4B in revenues, which is a 5% YoY growth of industrial revenues (FY23: EUR 45.3B). However, since the sales mix sees VW Truck & Bus reporting strong volume growth, it is not to take for granted that Traton's margins will be lower. In fact, MAN reported a RoS of 5.6% for the quarter, while VW reported 12.2%. If this trend is confirmed, then lower MAN's deliveries while VW sold more units might actually turn out to be beneficial for the company's profitability. So, I believe we will see a RoS above 9%, also supported by the strength Scania showed.

Valuation

Traton keeps being the cheapest stock in its industry, with a PE of 6.4, while Daimler Truck (OTCPK:DTRUY) trades at a 9.7 and Iveco (OTCPK:IVCGF) at a steep 11.1 that can be reasonable only in the case of Iveco spinning off its Defense unit or being acquired by a larger peer (some believe that the separation would ease any M&A involving Iveco itself). Paccar and Volvo trade at a 13 PE, which reflects a premium due to their leadership role in the industry.

Considering FY25 could be a flat year in comparison to FY24, we could have Traton reach once again EUR 47B in sales. Net income will probably be lower due to debt reduction (EUR 6.18B in LT industrial debt), so we could see a net income of around EUR 1.8B to EUR 2B. Traton has 500M shares outstanding, so the projection for its FY25 EPS is easy: EUR 3.80, assuming a net income of EUR 1.9B. The guidance for FY24 is around EUR 5.00, so we could be before a -24% YoY decrease. Currently, Traton trades at EUR 35.75 in Frankfurt, so the fwd 2025 PE could be 9.4. This positions Traton closer to its peers.

However, from the preliminary data, chances are Traton performed better than expected and could be off to a better start in 2025. If this is the case, then Traton is actually cheaper than what analysts expect. But we lack enough data to make a good guess. As a result, I am downgrading Traton to a hold into earnings, suggesting this is the moment to stay on the sidelines and watch things unfold a bit more.

data.title: Altria: Potential Beyond Traditional Tobacco Products Makes It A Must-Own For Dividend Investors
data.description:
Introduction

Most of us are well aware of the risks from the consistent and steady decline in smokeable products. And while this will continue to pose a threat to Altria's (NYSE:MO) dividend safety and peers alike, their robust growth in their smokeless products makes them a compelling dividend stock passive income investors should look to own.

Like every investment, owning a company at the right price is essential, and Altria, despite being up double-digits over the past year, could be poised to see further upside over the next 12–24 months due to additional growth drivers discussed later in the article.

During their latest earnings, Altria delivered what, I thought, was a solid earnings report to close out the year. And in this article, I discuss their latest report, financials, and why potential drivers of growth make them a must own stock for passive income investors.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Altria back in a November article titled: Altria: Sit Back And Continue To NJOY The Dividends. Since then, the stock has remained relatively flat, down slightly less than 1% in comparison to the S&P who has also seen a somewhat flat performance. Up less than 1%, the overall market seems to be dealing with uncertainty surrounding inflation.

As you can see in the chart below, MO continued their solid performance, trading near $58 a share before retracting back at the end of November and trading rangebound since.

Although smokeable products continued their steady decline during the third quarter, Altria showed strong price appreciation due to their strong growth of NJOY & on!

This allowed management to beat on both their top and bottom lines. EPS amounted to $1.38, beating estimates by $0.03 while revenue of $5.34 billion beat by a solid $10 million.

The cigarette giant's balance sheet was also strong with leverage of 2.1x, offsetting smokeable challenges and enhancing their dividend safety. Moreover, at a then forward P/E of 10.54x, Altria offered significant upside with a price target of roughly $74 if they returned to their normal P/E according to Fast Graphs.

Robust Smokeless Category Growth

During their most recent quarter, announced at the end of January, Altria again managed to beat on their top and bottom lines.

EPS of $1.29 declined from $1.38 in the previous quarter but beat analysts' estimates by a penny. Revenue of $5.1 billion also declined from $5.4 billion the previous quarter but beat estimates by a solid $50 million.

Peer Philip Morris (PM) saw a significantly higher beat by $270 million, driven by strong growth in their oral tobacco category.

As expected, smokeable products continued to trend downward, with revenues declining 0.2% and domestic volumes declining 10.2% for the full year. This was driven by expected lower shipment volumes, partially offset by higher pricing as seen in the chart below.

And while this will likely continue to pose risks for Altria for the foreseeable future, the company's robust growth in their smokeless products like NJOY & on! continues to show promise.

Other good news is that according to Altria's management, the number of adult consumers in the e-vapor and oral categories grew to roughly 28 million, on par with adult traditional smokers.

In the chart below, you can see the growth in revenue per capita expected over the next 5 years for e-cigarettes. This is expected to grow near 22% from 2025 to 2029.

And using Altria's distribution network, I expect both NJOY and on! to continue to see market share growth for the foreseeable future. Last year's fourth quarter saw Altria manage to distribute NJOY to over 75,000 stores, greatly enhancing their visibility and engagement with consumers.

Today, NJOY is in more than 100,000 stores. And volumes grew 15.3% year-over-year as a result to 12.8 million units and 46.6 million units respectively for the full-year. Devices saw even higher growth of more than 22% while consumables retail share increased 2.8 points from the prior year.

Oral nicotine pouches grew 9.6 points as a result of shipment volumes, which were up an impressive 44% with cans increasing from 30.4 million to 43.9 million.

For comparison purposes, Philip Morris saw shipment volumes grow from roughly 220 million to 275 million cans over the same period. Moreover, on! significantly trailed ZYN in terms of market share at 8.9% in comparison to 65.9%.

Potential Growth Drivers

And continuing their Moving Beyond Smoking campaign, management is still working towards their PTMA submission for Ploom through their joint venture with Japan Tobacco Inc. (OTCPK:JAPAY).

This is expected to be submitted by mid-2025. Helix also saw their first profitable quarter in Q4, and management expects this to continue going forward. And both could become significant growth drivers, resulting in strong price appreciation in the future.

So, although declines in smokeable products continue to weigh negatively on the company's share price, so does the large disparity in market share in comparison to Philip Morris' ZYN. But Altria seems to be on the right track to offset the continued decline in traditional smoking with volume and market share growth.

Earnings Growth & Buybacks

Altria's relentless share repurchases are another reason why they remain a favorite amongst dividend investors. And while this also helps grow their bottom line and somewhat offset the secular decline in traditional smoking, it also greatly enhances their dividend safety by saving millions annually by retiring shares.

Earnings were able to see solid growth of 9.3% from $1.18 to $1.29 year-over-year. This was in comparison to Philip Morris, who's earnings grew double-digits from $1.36 to $1.55 a share.

And while PM continues to outperform Altria in terms of price appreciation and smokeless tobacco product growth, Altria's frequent buybacks enables them to successfully navigate smoking challenges while returning ample cash to shareholders.

The company recently announced an additional $1 billion repurchase program that they intend to use by the end of the year. And EPS growth for 2025 is expected to be 3.4% from this year's $5.12 to their midpoint of $5.295.

And while this is low in comparison to PM's expectation of high single-digit growth over the next two years, Altria's yield and commitment to return cash to shareholders make them worth owning.

For 2024, the cigarette giant returned a total of $10.2 billion to shareholders, $6.8 billion of this in dividends. This was a much higher increase from $7.8 billion returned in 2023.

And still, free cash flow was sufficient enough to cover the $6.8 billion in dividends paid. With management repurchasing another $1 billion worth of shares this year, Altria's dividend remains safe for the near to medium term.

Debt

Altria's debt has also remained somewhat flat over the past few years. In 2022, long-term debt stood at $25.1 billion and roughly the same in 2023. Currently, this stands at $24.93 billion, and they had the lowest leverage ratio of their peer group at 2.1x. Philip Morris' net debt to EBITDA ratio was 2.66x, while British American Tobacco's (BTI) leverage was 2.6x.

Strong Total Returns/Risks

At a forward P/E of just 10.1x, Altria's dividend yield of nearly 8% and upside potential makes them a worthy investment for those in search of passive income. The stock has also significantly outperformed the S&P in total returns over the past year, delivering a solid 44% in comparison to 21% for the index.

Moreover, if interest rates decline this year, Altria should benefit, potentially seeing some upside by the end of the year. However, current risks from inflation as well as their lawsuit with JUUL will likely limit upside for the near term.

An order to stop the sale of NJOY ACE products was issued, ruling in JUUL's favor. MO's management appealed and is awaiting a decision that is expected to come in April of this year. If the decision is upheld, this could also result in downside for Altria.

Upside Potential

As stated previously, Altria has significant upside potential, especially if interest rates are lowered and the company is able to continue to see solid growth in NJOY & on! as well as additional growth drivers with Helix and Ploom.

Moreover, their dividend yield of nearly 8% is attractive and investors looking to get back into the market due to less appealing, fixed-rate investments will likely look to buy into the stock.

Using an expected 3% earnings growth rate, in-line with the past few years and 2.5% thereafter to account for any slowdowns in the economy or Altria's business, the cigarette giant offers solid upside of 16.7% at the time of writing.

Since Altria is a mature business and a higher yielding investment, I used an 11% expected return, slightly higher than the S&P's 7% - 10% historical average. However, as stated previously, upside will rely heavily on the direction of interest rates as well as their pending lawsuit.

Bottom Line

Altria, despite a strong performance, up over 32% in the past year, offers dividend investors great value with a forward multiple of less than 11x earnings.

And although current risks will likely weigh on the company's share price in the near term, on! and NJOY share growth continue to show Altria is positioning themselves for the consumer shift to smokeless tobacco products.

Moreover, potential growth drivers like Ploom and Helix could also be catalysts for the stock to see solid price appreciation over the long term along with the continued decline in interest rates. Altria's commitment to shareholder returns, attractive valuation, and potential growth drivers makes them a long-term buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 3 Of My Best Dividend Picks For A Huge Economic Shift
data.description:
Introduction

A 91% price surge since 2019. That sounds like horrible inflation, doesn't it?

Luckily, I'm talking about the Audemars Piguet price index, which tracks the weighted average price of this super-expensive Swiss watch brand. If you want a decent entry model and avoid the retail waitlist, you'll probably have to pay close to $50,000 (without any limits to the upside).

The good news is that this is the kind of inflation nobody really cares about. While it may be annoying for some that their favorite watch is now way more expensive, this isn't the kind of inflation that hurts communities.

If anything, it's a great indicator of market liquidity, as prices went through the roof when central bank liquidity propelled global asset prices during the pandemic. Since the Fed started hiking rates, that party has ended.

I'm bringing all of this up because not all inflation is equal.

While higher watch prices may be annoying, inflation in areas like food, housing, and energy, can be critical for many. The other day, I talked to someone who has been trying to find a new suitable house for him and his girlfriend for more than a year, as almost everything that isn't out of their financial reach comes with what seems to be unlimited buyer competition.

For example, all-item inflation seems to be bottoming at 3%. Rent inflation is still at 4%, a number even above the already elevated pre-pandemic levels.

Unfortunately, new data suggests that this could get worse, as my "higher for longer" thesis seems to get support from unfavorable housing developments - at least for renters and everyone looking to get into the market.

I believe the headline below speaks for itself:

After two years of falling inflation rates (not falling prices), the housing market is expected to see a toxic mix of low homeownership affordability, a rapidly depleting new construction pipeline, and elevated interest rates.

This will be a tough pill to swallow for consumers and another burden on the Fed, which is already in a tough spot due to rebounding inflation rates.

Rising rents would complicate the inflation picture and likely give the Federal Reserve another reason to pause on future rate cuts. Shelter costs account for roughly a third of the consumer-price index, which means that a significant portion of the overall inflation measure is attributed to housing costs.

“It will be very hard for the headline inflation number to reach the Fed’s 2% goal without a slowdown in housing costs,” said Lisa Sturtevant, chief economist at Bright MLS. - WSJ (emphasis added)

According to the Journal (which cites CoStar data), every single metropolitan market is expected to see positive rent growth by the end of this year.

As you can imagine, this has a number of implications:

  • Rent inflation will likely accelerate.
  • Much more new housing supply is needed to alleviate these pressures.
  • Politicians may push for looser regulations.
  • Labor demand will rise.

With all of this in mind, in light of these key developments, I present three dividend stocks that, I believe, will benefit tremendously from this.

I truly believe that they are among the best companies to invest in moving forward, as each brings something unique to the table, including innovative business models that benefit from more than just favorable macroeconomic trends.

While I do not own any of them, I am currently buying one for family accounts and likely for my own account after dealing with taxes and other matters in the next 4–6 weeks. The other two are on my watchlist.

So, let's dive into the picks!

Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) - I Believe It's Perfect

I have increasingly covered ELS this year, as I am starting to become a bit obsessed with the stock - in a good way.

We are currently buying ELS for family accounts. I will likely buy the stock after my trip to the U.S. when I have more clarity regarding taxes and issues I may have to deal with. Needless to say, I'll keep readers up to date.

For now, I want to do what I did in my February 9 article about suitable retirement stocks, which is to explain why Equity LifeStyle Properties is such a wickedly cool company.

Equity LifeStyle Properties is one of the best-performing REITs on the market, as it returned roughly 15% per year since going public, putting it in the best 10% of all public REITs over the past ten years.

The company, which has gone sideways since the pandemic, is the second-largest owner of manufactured housing ("MH") communities in the United States. It also owns RV communities and marinas. Put together, it generates 90% of its revenues from annual sources.

Even better, a big part of its 452 properties cater to the elderly. This is a huge benefit, as that group looks for affordable living arrangements with supportive amenities in places like California, Arizona, and Florida. It is also a fast-growing customer group due to the "Silver Tsunami." Between 2024 and 2039, the number of 55-year-olds in the U.S. is expected to rise by 15%.

Moreover, one major benefit of ELS is the increasing attractiveness of MH communities. Because of the increasing quality of manufactured houses and the company's amenities, MH communities are a real alternative for many people.

While upfront costs are usually higher, we see that total monthly costs are up to 70% lower for the typical MH compared to a single-family home.

But wait, it gets better.

On top of demand tailwinds, MH operators have supply tailwinds, which is quite unique in the real estate sector. As we can see below, supply has been constrained since 2008. Strict zoning and regulation have kept a lid on new developments, which is great for pricing power.

Additionally, 77% of ELS residents have a FICO score of more than 680. It has an occupancy rate of 95% with a homeownership ratio of almost 98%. This means that most people own their MH assets outright. They do not pay rent but monthly lot fees for the use of amenities.

Because of these qualities, the company has tremendous inflation protection, as most of its tenants have inflation-protected income and MH communities become increasingly more attractive when single-family affordability falls.

Since 2001, its net operating income growth has beaten inflation by almost 2.0% per year, which is a big deal.

The same goes for its dividend. Currently, ELS yields 3.2%. This yield comes with a five-year CAGR of 12.6% and an 80% payout ratio. Its most recent dividend hike was 7.9% on February 6.

Moreover, it has a healthy balance sheet with a net leverage ratio of just 4.6x EBITDA, an average of nine years to maturity, and a weighted average interest rate of 3.8%.

Looking ahead, analysts expect 4-6% average annual per-share adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO") growth. In other words, even without valuation tailwinds, the company could return close to 10%.

However, as ELS trades roughly four points below its 10-year average, it could return substantially more. Needless to say, I expect we need lower interest rates for that to happen.

Nonetheless, I believe the long-term risk/reward is fantastic, especially in light of more housing inflation.

While ELS does not yield 6% like some of the REITs you may have in your portfolio or on your radar, I believe it's one of the best dividend stocks money can buy - across all sectors.

D.R. Horton (DHI) - A Special Builder

There are two homebuilders I really like.

  • NVR (NVR).
  • D.R. Horton.

In this article, I'm covering D.R. Horton, as NVR does not pay a dividend.

NVR also does not have that one big benefit that D.R. Horton has, which is a big focus on affordable homes. It has also been the biggest homebuilder in America for 23 consecutive years, with a major footprint in 36 states.

Because demand for affordable homes has grown at a fast pace, the company's market share has risen from less than 3% in the early 1990s to almost 15% in 2024. This includes being the biggest builder in 61 markets, including many in its home state, Texas.

What's fascinating is that nearly 70% of its homes that were sold over the past four quarters had a price tag of less than $400,000. A quarter of its homes were delivered for less than $300,000. The median home price in the United States is north of $410,000.

On top of that, the company has mastered the art of building homes efficiently. After all, selling homes at a below-average price isn't rocket science. Doing it without sacrificing margins is where it becomes complicated.

However, the company beats its peers on three key metrics:

  • Return on equity.
  • Return on assets.
  • The 10-year total stock return.

During its 1Q25 (fiscal year) earnings call, the company noted that despite economic challenges, housing market conditions remain favorable for homebuilders, driven by strong demographic trends and the limited supply of affordable homes.

In order to deal with affordability concerns and to boost demand, D.R. Horton implemented targeted incentives, which included mortgage rate buy-downs and a strategic shift toward smaller floor plans.

With regard to its dividend, the company yields 1.2%. This dividend has a five-year CAGR of 17% and a payout ratio of a mere 9%. The reason behind that is the company's focus on buybacks, which have exceeded dividend payments by a wide margin since at least 2020.

While this dividend may be a bit disappointing, the total return has been fantastic over the past decade, as the company has consistently beaten the S&P 500 with total returns in the highest quartiles.

Valuation-wise, D.R. Horton trades at 9.5x earnings, a few points below its long-term average of 11.1x. While this fiscal year is expected to see an 11% EPS contraction, 2026 and 2027 are expected to see 12% and 13% EPS growth, respectively.

Personally, I believe these numbers will be higher if President Trump can avoid a recession and even lower rates a bit from current levels. In general, if the urge to build more homes grows, D.R. Horton is a very likely winner.

I believe this could unlock a fair stock price target of at least $180, roughly 37-40% above the current price and based on 2027 EPS expectations and its 11.1x long-term average P/E ratio.

The biggest risks are rising rates and Trump's mass deportations. Regardless of one's views on illegal immigration (I'm not getting political), deporting illegal labor could hurt the construction industry, as it's a source of cheap labor.

With that said, the next pick is similar, yet different due to a different place in the supply chain.

Boise Cascade Company (BCC) - Buying High-Quality Distribution

Boise Cascade is a company I would have bought if I had not already had a large position in its peer, Carlisle Companies (CSL), which I bought after the pandemic.

Boise Cascade is a fascinating company that benefits tremendously from inflation and housing shortages. Over the past five years, it has returned 332%, almost three times the return of the homebuilding ETF (ITB).

This company does not build homes. It's a supplier with a strong internal supply chain that includes two sawmills, 11 plywood/veneer plants, and 38 distribution facilities - among other key assets.

It uses its distribution facilities to sell its products to builders, remodelers, do-it-yourself customers, and industrial players. However, it is more than just a company that turns trees into building products, as it is increasingly expanding its distribution network to sell everything needed to build homes and other construction projects. This includes a wide range of brands that are all part of the wider distribution channel, as we can see below.

In 2023, the company generated $1.93 billion in revenues from its wood products division, with an EBITDA margin of 22.6%. That's up from 8.8% in 2019 and a confirmation of years of investments in higher efficiencies.

In its building materials segment, it generated close to $7.0 billion in sales, supported by a 9.9% average annual revenue growth since 2013. On top of secular growth tailwinds, it also benefits from size, as the company supplied 5% of all multi-family wood floors in 2023 and 13% of all multi-family I-joists, to give you two numbers.

Generally speaking, the company's focus on higher quality products is paying off, as the building industry is increasingly focused on products that reduce labor efforts (think of current deportations), improve cycle times, increase strength, and improve sustainability.

As much as I love builders, I love the distribution business, as it covers an entirely different area of the supply chain. I also expect AI and similar technologies to provide massive opportunities for efficiency improvements in this industry.

With regard to its dividend, BCC pays $0.21 per share per quarter. This translates to a yield of 0.7%. This dividend has an 8% payout ratio and a five-year CAGR of 17.3%.

However, it gets better, as the company pays special dividends.

Last year, it announced a $5.00 special dividend, which brings the total annualized yield to 4.8%. Although this special dividend is volatile, it has consistently risen over time, as we can see below.

Valuation-wise, we're dealing with a company trading at 12.6x earnings, below its ten-year average of 14.5x. Going forward, analysts expect 8%, 7%, and 17% EPS growth in 2025, 2026, and 2027, respectively.

This implies a fair stock price of $186, roughly 50% above the current price.

While all of these numbers are subject to change, I believe tailwinds are strong and provide a favorable risk/reward - even if interest rates remain elevated.

As I already said, the only reason I do not own BCC is because I own CSL. Because I will have to consolidate some positions before adding new investments, I decided to avoid buying more stocks in the same industry.

Takeaway

Not all inflation is created equal.

While luxury items like (Audemars Piguet) watches have seen staggering price surges, it's the inflation in essentials like housing, food, and energy that truly impacts lives.

The housing market in particular is facing a toxic mix of low affordability, falling supply, and high rates, which could keep rent inflation elevated.

Against this backdrop, I've identified three dividend stocks: Equity LifeStyle Properties, D.R. Horton, and Boise Cascade.

Each offers unique strengths, from affordable housing solutions to efficient supply chain distribution.

While I don't own all of them yet, I'm actively adding ELS to family accounts and keeping a close eye on the others.

Risks To My Thesis

Rising interest rates could further pressure housing affordability and slow demand, while economic downturns or policy changes (like mass deportations impacting construction labor) could disrupt supply chains and growth.

Additionally, if inflation in housing costs cools faster than expected or regulatory hurdles ease, the tailwinds driving these stocks may weaken.

Lastly, valuation multiples could remain depressed if market sentiment sours, meaning general market weakness could hurt these stocks.

data.title: Why Pfizer's Stock Price Isn't Reflecting Its True Value
data.description:
On February 4, Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) (NEOE:PFE:CA) released financial results for the fourth quarter of 2024, which again beat my expectations and those of Wall Street analysts.

Investment thesis

Pfizer, under the leadership of Albert Bourla, remains under pressure from critics, pessimists, and others, which is not surprising since its stock price is near multi-year lows, and Oxbryta, which was gaining popularity for the treatment of sickle cell anemia, was withdrawn from the market.

But is it really that bad?

Of course not.

In this article, you will not only read about the performance of its oncology, migraine, and cardiovascular franchises and its latest successes in developing a portfolio of experimental drugs, but also learn why I continue to cover Pfizer with a 'Strong Buy' rating.

Pfizer continues to beat Wall Street expectations, but its share price remains flat despite progress in R&D

Pfizer revenue was about $17.8 billion in the fourth quarter of 2024, up 24.9% year over year and 0.6% quarter over quarter.

In addition, looking at historical data, it has beaten consensus estimates by relatively large margins in each of the last four quarters, even as sales of Xeljanz [Janus kinase inhibitor] continue to decline due to the loss of its exclusivity in Canada and increased competition from AbbVie's Rinvoq (ABBV), Novartis' Cosentyx (NVS), and Johnson & Johnson's Stelara (JNJ).

Then, dear Seeking Alpha readers, you may have the first question, namely, what factors contributed to the beat of Wall Street analysts and my expectations?

So, Pfizer's revenue growth was driven by a sharp increase in sales of COVID-19 products [Comirnaty and Paxlovid], as well as increased demand for its oncology and tafamidis franchises.

Since Pfizer is most interested in oncology, it is logical that I will continue the discussion with an analysis of this franchise.

Sales of its oncology franchise reached about $4.06 billion in the fourth quarter of 2024, up 27.4% year-on-year and 0.5% quarter-on-quarter, mainly due to increased demand for its antibody-drug-conjugates, Xtandi, Elrexfio, Braftovi/Mektovi, and Elrexfio, which is a BCMA/CD3 bispecific antibody approved by the FDA in mid-August 2023 for the treatment of multiple myeloma.

The greatest weight in terms of sales relative to the revenue of this portfolio belongs to the medications that were acquired as part of the deal with Seagen.

So, the total sales of Tukysa, Tivdak, Adcetris, and Padcev amounted to $894 million, which is 7.1% more than in Q3 2024 and 92.7% more than in Q4 2022. Also, I would like to note that it was incorrect to compare the dynamics relative to the fourth quarter of 2023 because Pfizer, in its report, indicated only the sales of these 4 drugs for the period from the closing of the deal until the end of the year.

In my assessment, Xtandi (enzalutamide), which is a second-generation androgen receptor inhibitor [ARI], has also become a significant contributor to strengthening the balance sheet of the New York-based company.

Sales of the medication, developed jointly with Astellas (OTCPK:ALPMF), totaled $565 million in the fourth quarter, up 79.9% year-on-year, primarily due to its NMPA approval in early July 2024 for the treatment of patients with mHSPC [metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer].

The second factor contributing to the growth in demand for it was the approval by the EMA on January 8, 2024, in combination with Talzenna [PARP inhibitor]. The positive decision of the regulatory authority was based on the Phase 3 TALAPRO-2 study, in which the group of patients taking this combination had a 37% lower risk of death/disease progression compared to the placebo+Xtandi.

Moreover, on February 13, 2025, Pfizer reported additional clinical data that also pleased me because, in two cohorts of this pivotal trial, which has been going on for more than five years, Xtandi plus Talzenna led to a statistically significant increase in median overall survival relative to the combination of Xtandi with placebo.

Anticipating your question, dear Seeking Alpha readers. Who is the flagship in Pfizer's Specialty Care segment?

Of course, it is the tafamidis franchise. As I mentioned in the article "Pfizer: Tracking Well Above The Industry," it consists of three drugs, namely Vyndaqel [global], Vyndamax [U.S.], and Vynmac [Japan], which are approved for the treatment of cardiomyopathy. In simple terms, cardiomyopathy is a progressive disease that makes it harder for the heart to pump blood.

So, sales of the Vyndaqel family reached about $1.55 billion in Q4 2024, which is 60.8% more than a year earlier and 127.2% more than in the same period of 2022. This result beat my expectations by $120 million, since I believed that tougher competition from Alnylam Pharmaceuticals' Onpattro (ALNY) and BridgeBio Pharma's Attruby (BBIO) would somewhat slow demand for Pfizer's blockbuster.

From its Primary Care segment, I highlight Nurtec ODT/Vydura (rimegepant), a medication for the treatment of various types of migraines, which partially minimized the decline in sales of the RSV vaccine called Abrysvo.

I will indicate the reasons for the decline in demand for Abrysvo a little later, namely in the "risks" section.

Nurtec ODT/Vydura sales amounted to $392 million for the 3 months ended December 31, 2024, increasing by 39% year-on-year and by 16.3% quarter-on-quarter due to strong demand for it in the United States, including due to its competitive advantages relative to Amgen's Aimovig (AMGN), as well as its launch in international markets.

Let's continue with a discussion of the financial metric that I prefer more, namely earnings per share [EPS].

It also did not disappoint me, even though Pfizer continues to increase investments in the development of its FDA-approved and experimental medications, especially for those indications where there remain high unmet needs, including metastatic breast cancer, advanced solid tumors, metastatic non-small cell lung cancer, and lupus.

Which experimental drugs and vaccines do I single out as those that could restore Wall Street's faith in Pfizer's bright future as early as 2025?

From the list of its product candidates, which you can also see below, I highlight sasanlimab [bladder cancer], atirmociclib [breast cancer], vepdegestrant [breast cancer], and danuglipron [chronic weight management and type 2 diabetes], which, in my estimation, have significant commercial potential and could once again make Wall Street think about Pfizer in a positive way.

In addition, I want to highlight two points that pleased me as a Pfizer investor.

First, it terminated its partnership with Sangamo Therapeutics (SGMO) to develop giroctocogene fitelparvovec. I believe this decision was correct since this gene therapy for hemophilia A has demonstrated similar efficacy as BioMarin's Roctavian (BMRN), but its sales were poor, amounting to only $7 million in the third quarter of 2024.

Also, I believe that due to the long-acting medications, including Sanofi's Altuviiio (SNY) and Roche Holding's Hemlibra (OTCQX:RHHBY), it is unlikely that the medical community will choose to use expensive gene therapies.

The second point that strengthened my confidence in Pfizer's bright future is the words of Dave Denton, its CFO, on the Q4 earnings call. So, he said that the company has at least $10 billion that it could use for mergers and acquisitions.

However, dear Seeking Alpha readers, let's get back to discussing the dynamics of changes in Pfizer's EPS.

Its non-GAAP earnings per share [non-GAAP EPS] was 63 cents, up more than five times year-over-year and beating the analysts' consensus estimates by 17 cents. One factor that contributed to this was the more than successful implementation of a cost realignment program that began in 2023.

So, on the Q4 earnings call, Dave Denton, stated the following.

Importantly, we believe the steps taken to right size our cost base will put us on a strong footing towards increased operational efficiency and support our goal to return to pre-pandemic operating margins. In further support of this initiative, we now expect to deliver overall net savings of $4.5 billion from our ongoing cost realignment programs by the end of 2025, while continuing to advance programs that will improve cost of goods sold in the years to come.

Thanks to the wide range of data provided through Seeking Alpha, each of its readers, including me, can look a little into the future and assess how specific financial metrics of Pfizer will change.

Wall Street analysts forecast its non-GAAP EPS to be between 54 and 81 cents for the first three months of 2025, implying 12.7% quarterly growth. For comparison, I expect it to be 77 cents, which is above the midpoint of the above range due to stronger sales of antibody-drug conjugates used to treat various types of cancer, Nurtec ODT/Vydura, Zavicefta, Sulperazon and Cibinqo.

Risks

In the table below, I have highlighted the key risks related to Pfizer that, in my opinion, each of you, namely Seeking Alpha readers, should consider.

Takeaway

Dear Seeking Alpha readers, in this article, I presented an analysis of Pfizer's results for Q4 2024, my thoughts on its R&D policy, and successes and failures in developing an oncology franchise.

However, that's not all.

In addition to its high dividend yield of 6.74%, another factor that makes me a Pfizer investor and also why it is an attractive asset for long-term investors is that it trades at multiples that are significantly lower than those of its key competitors, including AstraZeneca (AZN) and Eli Lilly (LLY).

So, it trades at a P/E ratio [TTM] of 8.21x, which is 54.5% lower than the sector median and 23.4% lower than the average over the past 5 years. Besides, I estimate Pfizer's non-GAAP EPS to grow to $3.26, which corresponds to a P/E ratio of 7.33x in 2029.

data.title: Winter 2025 Snapshot Of Expected Future S&P 500 Earnings
data.description:
Every three months, we take a snapshot of the expectations for future earnings in the S&P 500 (SPX) at approximately the midpoint of the current quarter, shortly after most U.S. firms have announced their previous quarter's earnings.

The latest snapshot is a little early, but still 90 days since the Fall 2024 snapshot. During this time, remarkably little changed in the outlook for the collective earnings of the companies that make up the S&P 500 index. That relative lack of change is a new development that's taken place over the past six months.

That's remarkable because it runs counter to the pattern we typically see in how the outlook for earnings changes with time. That pattern is one in which the expectations for future earnings tend to erode with each later snapshot.

To be sure, that pattern holds in the Winter 2025 snapshot, but the amount of erosion is tiny. Looking at the S&P 500's anticipated earnings per share for the end of the fourth quarter of 2025, we find those expectations dipped by $1.49 per share. That's a decline of just 0.6%.

The following chart, covering how earnings expectations have changed from the end of 2021 through 11 February 2025 illustrates both the typical pattern and the relative lack of change in those expectations since 13 August 2024:

The current projection for the S&P 500's earnings per share through the end of 2025 is $249.13, which would represent 18.2% year-over-year earnings growth over December 2024's level of $210.81. Given the typical pattern for how earnings projections develop over time, that figure represents the likely ceiling for potential earnings growth during 2025.

The Winter 2025 snapshot also includes the first projection of the index's expected earnings per share through the end of 2026. The first estimate of what those earnings will be is $289.64 per share.

Reference

Silverblatt, Howard. Standard & Poor's. S&P 500 Earnings and Estimates. 13 November 2024. Accessed 17 February 2025.

Original Post
